topics.name: Explain the role and function of network components
subtopics: Routers
Layer 2 and Layer 3 switches
Next-generation firewalls and IPS
Access points
Controllers (WLC, Cisco DNA Center)
Endpoints
Servers

topics.name: Describe characteristics of network topology architectures
subtopics: Two-tier (collapsed core)
Three-tier (access, distribution, core)
Spine-leaf (data center fabric)
WAN
Small office/home office (SOHO)
On-premises and cloud

topics.name: Compare physical interface and cabling types
subtopics: Single-mode fiber, multimode fiber, copper
Connections (Ethernet shared media and point-to-point)

topics.name: Identify interface and cable issues (collisions, errors, mismatch duplex, and/or speed)
subtopics: Collisions and late collisions
CRC errors and frame errors
Runts (< 64 bytes) and giants (> 1518 bytes)
Input errors, output errors, and interface resets
Duplex mismatch — half vs full duplex
Speed mismatch and auto-negotiation failures
Interface status codes — up/up, up/down, down/down, admin down
Verify with show interfaces and show interfaces status

topics.name: Compare TCP to UDP
subtopics: TCP — connection-oriented, reliable, ordered delivery
TCP three-way handshake — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
TCP four-way teardown — FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK
TCP windowing, flow control, and sequencing
UDP — connectionless, best-effort, low overhead
TCP ports — FTP 20/21, SSH 22, Telnet 23, SMTP 25, HTTP 80, POP3 110, IMAP 143, HTTPS 443
UDP ports — DNS 53, DHCP 67/68, TFTP 69, NTP 123, SNMP 161/162, Syslog 514
Dual-protocol ports — DNS 53 (both TCP and UDP)

topics.name: Configure and verify IPv4 addressing and subnetting
subtopics: IPv4 address structure — 32-bit, dotted-decimal, network + host portions
Classful addressing — Class A /8 (1-126), B /16 (128-191), C /24 (192-223)
Subnet masks in dotted-decimal and CIDR slash notation
Calculating network address, broadcast address, and valid host range
Number of subnets (2^borrowed bits) and hosts per subnet (2^host bits - 2)
Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM) — different mask per subnet
Binary-to-decimal and decimal-to-binary conversion for subnetting
Verify — show ip interface brief, show ip route, ping

topics.name: Describe private IPv4 addressing
subtopics: RFC 1918 — private addresses require NAT to reach the internet
APIPA / link-local — 169.254.0.0/16 (auto-assigned when DHCP fails)
Loopback — 127.0.0.0/8 (127.0.0.1 for local testing)

topics.name: Configure and verify IPv6 addressing and prefix
subtopics: IPv6 format — 128-bit, 8 groups of 4 hex digits, colon-separated
Shortening rules — drop leading zeros, :: replaces one consecutive all-zero group
Prefix length — /64 for LAN subnets, /48 for site allocation, /128 for host route
SLAAC — host auto-generates address from RA prefix + EUI-64 or random interface ID
Stateless DHCPv6 — SLAAC for address, DHCPv6 for DNS/domain info only
Stateful DHCPv6 — full address assignment from DHCPv6 server
NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) — RS, RA, NS, NA replace ARP
Configure — ipv6 address [addr/prefix], ipv6 enable, ipv6 unicast-routing

topics.name: Describe IPv6 address types
subtopics: Unicast — global (2000::/3), unique local (FC00::/7), link-local (FE80::/10)
Anycast — same address on multiple devices, routed to nearest
Multicast — FF00::/8, replaces broadcast (FF02::1 = all nodes, FF02::2 = all routers)
Modified EUI-64 — insert FFFE in MAC, flip 7th bit to form interface ID

topics.name: Verify IP parameters for Client OS (Windows, Mac OS, Linux)
subtopics: Windows — ipconfig, ipconfig /all, ipconfig /release, ipconfig /renew
Linux — ip addr, ip route, ifconfig (legacy), ss, nmcli
macOS — ifconfig, networksetup -getinfo, System Preferences > Network
Verify gateway, subnet mask, DNS server, DHCP lease info
Troubleshoot — ping (reachability), traceroute/tracert (path), nslookup/dig (DNS)

topics.name: Describe wireless principles
subtopics: Nonoverlapping Wi-Fi channels — 2.4 GHz: 1, 6, 11; 5 GHz: many non-overlapping
SSID — network name broadcast by AP, can be hidden
RF — frequency bands, interference, signal strength (RSSI), SNR
Encryption — WEP (broken), WPA (TKIP), WPA2 (AES), WPA3 (SAE)

topics.name: Explain virtualization fundamentals (server virtualization, containers, and VRFs)
subtopics: Type 1 hypervisor — bare metal (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM), runs directly on hardware
Type 2 hypervisor — hosted (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation), runs on top of OS
Virtual machines — full OS per VM, hardware-level isolation, heavier
Containers — share host kernel, lightweight, fast startup (Docker)
VRF — multiple independent routing tables on one physical router
VRF-Lite — VRF without MPLS, used for traffic segmentation on campus

topics.name: Describe switching concepts
subtopics: MAC learning — switch records source MAC + ingress port in MAC table
Frame switching — known unicast forwarded out the correct port only
Frame flooding — unknown unicast, broadcast, and multicast flooded to all ports except source
MAC address table — aging timer (default 300s), show mac address-table

topics.name: Configure and verify VLANs (normal range) spanning multiple switches
subtopics: Access ports (data and voice) — switchport mode access, switchport access vlan
Default VLAN (VLAN 1) — all ports start here, cannot be deleted
InterVLAN connectivity — requires L3 device (router-on-a-stick or L3 switch SVI)

topics.name: Configure and verify interswitch connectivity
subtopics: Trunk ports — carry multiple VLANs between switches
802.1Q — inserts 4-byte VLAN tag into Ethernet frame
Native VLAN — untagged traffic on trunk (default VLAN 1, should match both sides)

topics.name: Configure and verify Layer 2 discovery protocols (Cisco Discovery Protocol and LLDP)
subtopics: CDP — Cisco proprietary, Layer 2, enabled by default on Cisco devices
CDP commands — show cdp neighbors, show cdp neighbors detail, show cdp entry *
CDP enable/disable — cdp run (global), cdp enable (interface), no cdp run/enable
LLDP — IEEE 802.1AB open standard, disabled by default on Cisco
LLDP commands — show lldp neighbors, show lldp neighbors detail
LLDP enable/disable — lldp run (global), lldp transmit + lldp receive (per interface)
CDP vs LLDP — CDP is Cisco-only, on by default; LLDP is standard, off by default

topics.name: Configure and verify (Layer 2/Layer 3) EtherChannel (LACP)
subtopics: EtherChannel — bundles parallel links into one logical channel (bandwidth + redundancy)
LACP (802.3ad) — open standard; modes: active (initiates) and passive (responds)
PAgP — Cisco proprietary; modes: desirable (initiates) and auto (responds)
Static (mode on) — no negotiation protocol, both sides must be on
Negotiation matrix — LACP: active+active or active+passive; PAgP: desirable+desirable or desirable+auto
Layer 2 EtherChannel — switchport mode, interface port-channel as L2
Layer 3 EtherChannel — no switchport, assign IP to port-channel interface
Requirements — same speed, duplex, VLAN config, trunk settings on all member ports
Verify — show etherchannel summary, show etherchannel port-channel

topics.name: Interpret basic operations of Rapid PVST+ Spanning Tree Protocol
subtopics: Root port, root bridge (primary/secondary), and other port names
Port states — discarding, learning, forwarding (Rapid PVST+ has 3 states)
PortFast — immediately transitions to forwarding (access ports only, never on trunks)
Root guard — prevents unauthorized root bridge; BPDU guard — shuts port on BPDU receipt
STP election — lowest bridge ID wins (priority + MAC), default priority 32768
STP cost — 10 Gbps=2, 1 Gbps=4, 100 Mbps=19, 10 Mbps=100 (revised IEEE)
Verify — show spanning-tree, show spanning-tree vlan [id]

topics.name: Describe Cisco Wireless Architectures and AP modes
subtopics: Autonomous AP — standalone, self-contained config, each AP managed individually
Lightweight AP — managed by WLC via CAPWAP tunnel (control UDP 5246, data UDP 5247)
Split-MAC — AP handles real-time (RF, encryption); WLC handles management (auth, roaming, QoS)
FlexConnect — lightweight AP that locally switches traffic when WLC is unreachable
Cloud-based — Cisco Meraki, managed via cloud dashboard, no on-prem controller
Cisco DNA Center — SDN controller for campus wireless and wired (intent-based networking)

topics.name: Describe physical infrastructure connections of WLAN components (AP, WLC, access/trunk ports, and LAG)
subtopics: AP connects to switch access port (single VLAN) or trunk port (multiple WLANs)
WLC connects to switch via trunk port — carries management + all WLAN VLANs
LAG — WLC uplinks bundled for bandwidth and redundancy
PoE — APs powered over Ethernet (802.3af/at), eliminates separate power cabling

topics.name: Describe network device management access (Telnet, SSH, HTTP, HTTPS, console, TACACS+/RADIUS, and cloud managed)
subtopics: Console — RJ-45/USB-mini, out-of-band, direct serial connection, no network required
Telnet — TCP 23, in-band, sends everything in plaintext (insecure, avoid)
SSH — TCP 22, in-band, encrypted (always prefer over Telnet)
HTTP — TCP 80, web GUI management (insecure); HTTPS — TCP 443, encrypted web GUI
TACACS+ — TCP 49, Cisco proprietary, full packet encryption, per-command authorization
RADIUS — UDP 1812/1813, open standard, encrypts password only, combines auth+authz
Cloud managed — Meraki dashboard, Cisco Spaces, DNA Center (no direct CLI needed)

topics.name: Interpret the wireless LAN GUI configuration for client connectivity, such as WLAN creation, security settings, QoS profiles, and advanced settings
subtopics: WLAN creation — set name, SSID, WLAN ID, enable/disable
Security settings — Layer 2: WPA+WPA2, cipher: AES; auth: PSK or 802.1X
Interface/VLAN mapping — associate WLAN to a dynamic interface (VLAN)
QoS profiles — Platinum (voice), Gold (video), Silver (best effort), Bronze (background)
Advanced — client exclusion, P2P blocking, session timeout, DHCP required

topics.name: Interpret the components of routing table
subtopics: Routing protocol code — C (connected), S (static), O (OSPF), R (RIP), D (EIGRP), B (BGP)
Prefix — network address (e.g., 10.1.1.0)
Network mask — subnet mask or prefix length (e.g., /24 or 255.255.255.0)
Next hop — IP address of next router in the path
Administrative distance — trustworthiness of route source (lower = more trusted)
Metric — cost within a routing protocol (lower = preferred)
Gateway of last resort — default route (0.0.0.0/0), used when no specific match

topics.name: Determine how a router makes a forwarding decision by default
subtopics: Longest prefix match — most specific route wins (/30 beats /24 beats /16 beats /0)
Administrative distance (AD) — tiebreaker between different routing sources
AD values — Connected 0, Static 1, eBGP 20, EIGRP 90, OSPF 110, IS-IS 115, RIP 120, eBGP 200
Routing protocol metric — tiebreaker within same protocol (lower metric preferred)
Decision order — 1st longest match, 2nd lowest AD, 3rd lowest metric

topics.name: Configure and verify IPv4 and IPv6 static routing
subtopics: Default route — ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop | exit-interface]
Network route — ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop | exit-interface]
Host route — ip route [host] 255.255.255.255 [next-hop] (/32 prefix)
Floating static — static route with higher AD as backup (e.g., ip route ... 5)
IPv6 static — ipv6 route [prefix/length] [next-hop | exit-interface]
Next-hop vs exit-interface — next-hop is recursive (2 lookups); exit-interface is directly connected
Verify — show ip route static, show ip route [network], show ipv6 route

topics.name: Configure and verify single area OSPFv2
subtopics: Neighbor adjacencies — matching Hello/Dead timers, area ID, subnet, auth
Point-to-point — no DR/BDR election, two routers only
Broadcast (DR/BDR selection) — highest priority wins, then highest router ID
Router ID — manually set (router-id) > highest loopback IP > highest physical IP
Config — router ospf [id], network [ip] [wildcard] area [#], or ip ospf [id] area [#] on interface
Passive interface — passive-interface [int] stops Hello packets but still advertises the network
OSPF cost — reference BW / interface BW; change with auto-cost reference-bandwidth [Mbps]
Default route — default-information originate (advertises 0.0.0.0/0 into OSPF)
Verify — show ip ospf neighbor, show ip ospf interface [int], show ip route ospf

topics.name: Describe the purpose, functions, and concepts of first hop redundancy protocols
subtopics: FHRP purpose — virtual default gateway shared between routers for failover
HSRP (Cisco) — active/standby roles, virtual IP, virtual MAC 0000.0c07.acXX
HSRPv2 — supports IPv6, virtual MAC 0000.0c9f.fXXX, multicast 224.0.0.102
VRRP (open standard) — master/backup roles, uses actual interface IP as virtual IP allowed
GLBP (Cisco) — AVG assigns virtual MACs to AVFs, provides load balancing across routers
Preemption — higher-priority router reclaims active/master role when it recovers

topics.name: Configure and verify inside source NAT using static and pools
subtopics: NAT terminology — inside local, inside global, outside local, outside global
Static NAT — ip nat inside source static [local-ip] [global-ip] (one-to-one)
Dynamic NAT — ip nat pool [name] [start] [end] netmask [mask] + ip nat inside source list [#] pool [name]
PAT (overload) — add 'overload' keyword; many inside hosts share one public IP via port numbers
Interface designations — ip nat inside on LAN interface, ip nat outside on WAN interface
ACL for NAT — access-list defines which inside hosts get translated
Verify — show ip nat translations, show ip nat statistics
Troubleshoot — debug ip nat, clear ip nat translation *, check ACL + interface designations

topics.name: Configure and verify NTP operating in a client and server mode
subtopics: NTP purpose — synchronize clocks for log correlation, certificates, authentication
Stratum — 0 (atomic clock), 1 (directly connected), 2-15 (each hop +1), 16 (unsynchronized)
NTP client — ntp server [ip-address] (points to upstream server)
NTP master — ntp master [stratum] (device acts as NTP server)
NTP authentication — ntp authenticate, ntp authentication-key [#] md5 [key], ntp trusted-key [#]
Verify — show ntp status (synchronized?), show ntp associations, show clock

topics.name: Explain the role of DHCP and DNS within the network
subtopics: DHCP DORA — Discover (broadcast), Offer (unicast), Request (broadcast), Acknowledge (unicast)
DHCP provides — IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS server, lease time
DHCP lease renewal — at 50% of lease (unicast to server), rebind at 87.5% (broadcast)
DNS record types — A (name→IPv4), AAAA (name→IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), PTR (reverse), NS (nameserver)
DNS resolution — recursive (client→resolver does full lookup) vs iterative (resolver→root→TLD→auth)
DNS hierarchy — root (.) → TLD (.com) → second-level (example.com) → host (www.example.com)

topics.name: Explain the function of SNMP in network operations
subtopics: SNMP architecture — manager (NMS polls), agent (on device responds), MIB (object database)
SNMP messages — Get (read one), GetNext (walk tree), GetBulk (many), Set (write), Trap (unsolicited alert), Inform (acknowledged trap)
SNMPv2c — community strings (read-only / read-write), no encryption, no real auth
SNMPv3 security levels — noAuthNoPriv (username only), authNoPriv (MD5/SHA auth), authPriv (auth + DES/AES encryption)
SNMPv3 is the only version with encryption — always prefer v3 for production

topics.name: Describe the use of syslog features, including facilities and severity levels
subtopics: Level 0 — Emergency: system is unusable
Level 1 — Alert: immediate action needed
Level 2 — Critical: critical conditions
Level 3 — Error: error conditions
Level 4 — Warning: warning conditions
Level 5 — Notification: normal but significant
Level 6 — Informational: informational messages
Level 7 — Debugging: debug-level messages
Mnemonic — Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice cream Daily
Logging destinations — console, terminal monitor, buffer (RAM), remote syslog server
Config — logging host [ip], logging trap [level], logging buffered [size], logging console [level]

topics.name: Configure and verify DHCP client and relay
subtopics: DHCP client — ip address dhcp on router interface
DHCP relay — ip helper-address [server-ip] on the gateway interface facing clients
Why relay needed — DHCP Discover is broadcast, routers don't forward broadcasts by default
DHCP server config — ip dhcp pool [name], network [ip] [mask], default-router [gw], dns-server [ip]
Excluded addresses — ip dhcp excluded-address [start-ip] [end-ip] (reserve IPs for static devices)
Verify — show ip dhcp binding, show ip dhcp pool, show ip dhcp server statistics

topics.name: Explain the forwarding per-hop behavior (PHB) for QoS such as classification, marking, queuing, congestion, policing, and shaping
subtopics: Classification — identifying traffic (ACL, NBAR, port number, DSCP value)
Marking — tagging packets: Layer 2 CoS (0-7, 3 bits), Layer 3 DSCP (0-63, 6 bits)
DSCP values — EF (46, voice), AF classes (AF11-AF43), CS values (CS0-CS7), default BE (0)
Queuing — LLQ (strict priority for voice/video), CBWFQ (bandwidth guarantees per class)
Congestion avoidance — WRED drops random packets before queue is full, prevents tail drop
Policing — drops or remarks excess traffic immediately (hard limit, typically at ingress)
Shaping — buffers excess traffic and sends when bandwidth available (smooth, typically at egress)
Trust boundary — where markings are first trusted; typically at the access switch closest to source

topics.name: Configure network devices for remote access using SSH
subtopics: Step 1 — hostname [name] (cannot generate RSA keys with default hostname)
Step 2 — ip domain-name [domain] (required for RSA key generation)
Step 3 — crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048 (minimum 768 for SSHv2)
Step 4 — ip ssh version 2 (enforce version 2, more secure)
Step 5 — username [name] privilege 15 secret [password] (local user account)
Step 6 — line vty 0 15 → transport input ssh → login local
Disable Telnet — transport input ssh (replaces default 'transport input all')
Verify — show ip ssh (version, timeout), show ssh (active sessions)

topics.name: Describe the capabilities and functions of TFTP/FTP in the network
subtopics: TFTP — UDP 69, no authentication, simple, connectionless, for small trusted transfers
FTP — TCP 20 (data) + 21 (control), username/password auth, reliable, for larger transfers
TFTP use case — IOS image backup/upgrade, config file transfer on trusted networks
FTP use case — larger file transfers needing authentication and reliability
IOS file commands — copy running-config tftp:, copy tftp: flash:, copy startup-config tftp:
SCP — Secure Copy over SSH, encrypted alternative to TFTP/FTP

topics.name: Define key security concepts (threats, vulnerabilities, exploits, and mitigation techniques)
subtopics: Threat — potential danger (attacker, malware, natural disaster)
Vulnerability — exploitable weakness (unpatched software, misconfiguration, default credentials)
Exploit — code or technique that takes advantage of a vulnerability
Mitigation — action to reduce risk (patching, ACLs, encryption, segmentation, training)
Phishing and social engineering — manipulate humans to gain access
DoS / DDoS — overwhelm target with traffic to deny service
Man-in-the-middle — intercept communication between two parties (ARP spoofing, DHCP spoofing)
Malware — virus (attaches to file), worm (self-spreads), trojan (disguised), ransomware (encrypts data)

topics.name: Describe security program elements (user awareness, training, and physical access control)
subtopics: User awareness — recognize phishing, report suspicious emails, password hygiene
Security training — ongoing education, policy compliance, incident response procedures
Physical access — badge readers, mantraps/vestibules, security guards
Surveillance — video cameras, motion sensors, access logs
Environmental — cable locks, equipment cages, locked server rooms

topics.name: Configure and verify device access control using local passwords
subtopics: enable password [pw] — stored plaintext in config (never use)
enable secret [pw] — stored as MD5 hash (Type 5), always preferred over enable password
Console — line console 0 → password [pw] → login
VTY — line vty 0 15 → password [pw] → login (or login local with username)
AUX — line aux 0 → password [pw] → login
Local user — username [name] privilege [0-15] secret [pw] → login local on lines
service password-encryption — encrypts all plaintext passwords as Type 7 (weak but not plaintext)
Banners — banner motd # [text] #, banner login # [text] #, banner exec # [text] #
Minimum length — security passwords min-length [n]
Login protection — login block-for [sec] attempts [n] within [sec]

topics.name: Describe security password policy elements, such as management, complexity, and password alternatives (multifactor authentication, certificates, and biometrics)
subtopics: Password complexity — minimum length, uppercase + lowercase + numbers + special characters
Password management — rotation policy, expiration, history (no reuse)
MFA — something you know (password) + something you have (token) + something you are (biometric)
True MFA requires factors from DIFFERENT categories (two passwords is NOT MFA)
Digital certificates — PKI, X.509, CA (Certificate Authority), public/private key pairs
Biometrics — fingerprint, facial recognition, iris scan, voice print

topics.name: Describe IPsec remote access and site-to-site VPNs
subtopics: Site-to-site VPN — router-to-router, connects two networks, always-on
Remote access VPN — client-to-router, connects user to network, on-demand
IPsec provides — confidentiality (encryption), integrity (hashing), authentication (pre-shared key or cert), anti-replay
AH (Authentication Header) — integrity + auth only, NO encryption, IP protocol 51
ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) — encryption + integrity + auth, IP protocol 50
IKE Phase 1 — authenticate peers, establish ISAKMP SA (secure management channel)
IKE Phase 2 — negotiate IPsec SAs for actual data encryption (Quick Mode)
Tunnel mode — encrypts ENTIRE original packet + new IP header (site-to-site default)
Transport mode — encrypts payload ONLY, keeps original IP header (host-to-host)
GRE over IPsec — GRE provides multicast/routing protocol support, IPsec provides encryption

topics.name: Configure and verify access control lists
subtopics: Standard ACL — filters source IP only; numbered 1-99, 1300-1999
Extended ACL — filters source, dest, protocol, port; numbered 100-199, 2000-2699
Named ACLs — ip access-list standard/extended [name] (easier to edit)
Wildcard masks — inverse of subnet mask; 0 bits = must match, 1 bits = ignore
Wildcard examples — 0.0.0.0 = exact host, 0.0.0.255 = match /24, 0.0.255.255 = match /16
Implicit deny any — invisible 'deny any' at end of every ACL; must add permit statements
Processing — top-down, first match stops processing; order matters
Standard placement — near destination (only filters source, so place far from source)
Extended placement — near source (has full granularity, block early)
Apply to interface — ip access-group [name/#] in|out
Apply to VTY lines — access-class [name/#] in (restrict remote management access)
Edit — use sequence numbers; no [seq#] to remove, [seq#] permit/deny to insert
Verify — show access-lists, show ip access-lists, show running-config | include access

topics.name: Configure and verify Layer 2 security features (DHCP snooping, dynamic ARP inspection, and port security)
subtopics: DHCP snooping purpose — blocks rogue DHCP servers on untrusted ports
DHCP snooping config — ip dhcp snooping → ip dhcp snooping vlan [#] → ip dhcp snooping trust on uplinks
DHCP snooping binding table — maps MAC, IP, VLAN, port, lease time (used by DAI)
DHCP snooping rate limiting — ip dhcp snooping limit rate [pps] on untrusted ports
DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection) — validates ARP packets against DHCP snooping binding table
DAI config — ip arp inspection vlan [#] → ip arp inspection trust on uplinks/DHCP server ports
Port security purpose — limits MAC addresses allowed on a port
Port security config — switchport port-security → switchport port-security maximum [n]
Sticky MAC — switchport port-security mac-address sticky (auto-learns and saves to running-config)
Violation modes — protect (drop silently), restrict (drop + log + syslog), shutdown (err-disabled)
Err-disabled recovery — shutdown then no shutdown, or errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation

topics.name: Compare authentication, authorization, and accounting concepts
subtopics: Authentication — who are you? Verify identity (username/password, certificate, biometric)
Authorization — what can you do? Grant or deny access to resources and commands
Accounting — what did you do? Log all actions for audit trail
TACACS+ — TCP 49, Cisco proprietary, encrypts FULL packet, separates all 3 AAA functions
RADIUS — UDP 1812 (auth) / 1813 (acct), open standard, encrypts PASSWORD only, combines auth+authz
Use case — TACACS+ for device admin (per-command authz); RADIUS for network access (802.1X, VPN)

topics.name: Describe wireless security protocols (WPA, WPA2, and WPA3)
subtopics: WEP — RC4 encryption, 24-bit IV, trivially cracked, never use
WPA — TKIP encryption (improved WEP), deprecated, still crackable
WPA2 — AES/CCMP encryption, current required standard
WPA3 — SAE (replaces 4-way handshake, immune to offline dictionary attacks), OWE for open networks, 192-bit mode
Personal mode (PSK) — one pre-shared key for all users, simpler but less secure
Enterprise mode (802.1X) — individual credentials via RADIUS server, per-user auth

topics.name: Configure and verify WLAN within the GUI using WPA2 PSK
subtopics: Access WLC — HTTPS to management IP address in browser
Create WLAN — WLANs tab → Create New → set profile name, SSID, WLAN ID
Security — Layer 2 Security → WPA+WPA2 → AES cipher → Auth Key Mgmt: PSK → enter key
Interface mapping — General tab → select dynamic interface (maps WLAN to VLAN)
Enable — check Status: Enabled checkbox → Apply → verify client association

topics.name: Explain how automation impacts network management
subtopics: Reduced human error — automated templates eliminate typos and inconsistency
Faster provisioning — deploy configs to hundreds of devices in seconds, not hours
Scalability — manage thousands of devices from a single controller/script
Consistency and compliance — enforced standards, auditable changes, version control
Self-healing networks — automated detection and remediation of failures

topics.name: Compare traditional networks with controller-based networking
subtopics: Traditional — distributed control plane per device, CLI-based, manual, vendor-specific
Controller-based (SDN) — centralized control plane, API-driven, programmable
SDN benefits — single pane of glass, faster changes, policy-based, intent-based
Cisco DNA Center — SDN controller for enterprise campus (intent-based networking)

topics.name: Describe controller-based, software defined architecture (overlay, underlay, and fabric)
subtopics: Separation of control plane and data plane
Northbound APIs — controller to applications (REST API); Southbound APIs — controller to devices (OpenFlow, NETCONF)
Underlay — physical network providing transport (routers, switches, cables)
Overlay — virtual network on top of underlay (VXLAN tunnels, encapsulation)
Fabric — combined overlay + underlay with automated provisioning (Cisco SD-Access)

topics.name: Explain AI (generative and predictive) and machine learning in network operations
subtopics: Generative AI — creates configs from natural language, generates troubleshooting suggestions
Predictive AI — anomaly detection, capacity forecasting, failure prediction before impact
ML-driven monitoring — learns baseline behavior, auto-detects deviations
AIOps — automated root cause analysis, event correlation, intelligent alerting

topics.name: Describe characteristics of REST-based APIs (authentication types, CRUD, HTTP verbs, and data encoding)
subtopics: REST principles — stateless, client-server, URI identifies resources, cacheable
HTTP verbs and CRUD — GET (Read), POST (Create), PUT (Replace), PATCH (Update), DELETE (Delete)
Status codes — 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error
Authentication — API key, Bearer token, OAuth 2.0, HTTP Basic (base64 user:pass)
Data encoding — JSON (primary, lightweight), XML (legacy, verbose), YAML (config files)
CRUD mapping — Create=POST, Read=GET, Update=PUT/PATCH, Delete=DELETE

topics.name: Recognize the capabilities of configuration management mechanisms such as Ansible and Terraform
subtopics: Ansible — agentless (SSH/NETCONF), YAML playbooks, push model, idempotent
Ansible components — inventory (device list), playbooks (tasks), modules (actions), roles (reusable)
Terraform — IaC (Infrastructure as Code), HCL language, state file tracks real-world resources
Terraform workflow — terraform init → plan (preview) → apply (execute) → destroy (teardown)
Ansible vs Terraform — Ansible configures devices (day 2); Terraform provisions infrastructure (day 0/1)
Puppet/Chef (comparison) — agent-based, pull model, Ruby-based; less common on CCNA but may appear

topics.name: Recognize components of JSON-encoded data
subtopics: JSON object — { } curly braces, key-value pairs, keys MUST be double-quoted strings
JSON array — [ ] square brackets, ordered list of values
Data types — string ("text"), number (42, 3.14), boolean (true/false), null, object, array
Nesting — objects inside objects, arrays inside objects, objects inside arrays
Valid vs invalid — double quotes required (not single), no trailing commas, no comments allowed
